message.reasoning_content:
We are asked to validate extracted ASHRAE Guideline 36 items. We must output JSON with verdicts for each candidate_id. For each, determine if it is valid Guideline 36 knowledge (true) or not (false). Accept if useful, grounded operational knowledge. Reject legal/front matter, TOC text, generic definitions without operational use, duplicated/noisy extraction, or summaries not supported by evidence.

We need to check each candidate. All candidates appear to be from section 5.5 (VAV Terminal Unit Control). They have evidence quotes from pages. The evidence must support the summary and be from the guideline. We should check consistency.

Candidate cand_3eb93d212c60d3f2: title "Recommendation for reheat when minimum ventilation is high", section 5.5.3. Evidence: "If the minimum ventilation rate is more than 25% or so of the cooling maximum, or DCV is used, a reheat box is recommended to avoid overcooling. DCV logic is not provided for cooling-only boxes, because doing so results in periods of overcooling, as the CO2 levels due to occupants rises much faster than the cooling load due to occupants because of thermal mass." This is a recommendation, operational guidance. Valid.

Candidate cand_3d7d90fd86d1d8f6: title "Heating capability condition for cooling-only terminals", section 5.5.3. Evidence: "Cooling-only terminal units can provide heating only when the AHU supply air temperature is more than 3°C (5°F) above the room temperature." This is an operational condition. Valid.

Candidate cand_6655e2a0cbbf7099: title "Airflow setpoint mapping in cooling state", section 5.5.5.1. Evidence: "5.5.5.1. When the Zone State is cooling, the cooling-loop output shall be mapped to the active airflow setpoint from the minimum endpoint to the cooling maximum endpoint. a. If supply air temperature from the air handler is greater than room temperature, the active airflow setpoint shall be no higher than the minimum endpoint." Clear operational sequence. Valid.

Candidate cand_251fa8347372dafc: title "Airflow setpoint in deadband state", section 5.5.5.2. Evidence: "5.5.5.2. When the Zone State is deadband, the active airflow setpoint shall be the minimum endpoint." Valid.

Candidate cand_2746a6fb9fbdb121: title "Airflow setpoint mapping in heating state", section 5.5.5.3. Evidence: "5.5.5.3. When the Zone State is heating, the Heating Loop output shall be mapped to the active airflow setpoint from the minimum endpoint to the heating maximum endpoint. a. If supply air temperature from the air handler is less than 3°C (5°F) above the room temperature, the active airflow setpoint shall be no higher than the minimum endpoint." Valid.

Candidate cand_a383652c7a0f6fe5: title "VAV damper modulation for airflow control", section 5.5.5.4. Evidence: "5.5.5.4. The VAV damper shall be modulated by a control loop to maintain the measured airflow at the active setpoint." Valid.

Candidate cand_d76ddb5e8e894b19: title "Low airflow Level 4 alarm", section 5.5.6.1. Evidence: "a. If the measured airflow is less than 70% of setpoint for 10 minutes while setpoint is greater than zero, generate a Level 4 alarm." This is a fault diagnostic rule. Note: The evidence is from section 5.5.6.1? The source pages given are [77, 81, 86, 89, 93, 98, 102, 106, 109]. But the candidate sources one page. That's fine. Valid.

Candidate cand_8913cae55b377b74: title "Low airflow Level 3 alarm", section 5.5.6.1. Evidence: "b. If the measured airflow is less than 50% of setpoint for 10 minutes while setpoint is greater than zero, generate a Level 3 alarm." Valid.

Candidate cand_adfb9e8642a28b65: title "Suppression of low airflow alarms for zones with importance multiplier 0", section 5.5.6.1. Evidence: "c. If a zone has an importance multiplier of 0 (see Section 5.1.14.2.a.1) for its static pressure reset T&R control loop, low airflow alarms shall be suppressed for that zone." Valid.

Candidate cand_ea8b44c1a31e19b2: title "Airflow sensor calibration fault", section 5.5.6.2. Evidence: "If the fan serving the zone is OFF and airflow sensor reading is above the larger of 10% of Vcool-max or 50 cfm for 30 minutes, generate a Level 3 alarm." Valid.

Candidate cand_1e2dc344e5094a66: title "Leaking damper detection", section 5.5.6.3. Evidence: "If the damper position is 0%, and airflow sensor reading is above the larger of 10% of the cooling maximum airflow setpoint or 50 cfm for 10 minutes while the fan serving the zone is proven on, generate a Level 4 alarm." Valid.

Candidate cand_2f829e63c44a51c1: title "Airflow alarm threshold determination", section 5.5.6.3? Evidence: "The constant value thresholds for the airflow sensor calibration and leaking damper alarms are a function of the transducer and A/D converter used to measure airflow. The value used should be determined as the minimum accuracy of the transducer and A/D converter combination." This is application guidance, valid.

Candidate cand_c41c590069000678: title "Commissioning override switches", section 5.5.7. Evidence: "5.5.7. Testing/Commissioning Overrides. Provide software switches that interlock to a system-level point to a. force zone airflow setpoint to zero, b. force zone airflow setpoint to Vcool-max, c. force zone airflow setpoint to Vmin, d. force damper full closed/open, and e. reset request-hours accumulator point to zero (provide one point for each reset type listed in the next section)." This is a commissioning step, valid.

Candidate cand_640de17e4ddea1b5: title "Recommendation to include heating with CO2 DCV", section 5.5. Evidence: "CO 2 DCV for cooling-only zones can lead to overcooling due to the faster rise in CO 2 levels from people in the room versus the increase in cooling loads from people. Including heat in all zones with CO 2 DCV is therefore recommended." This might be a duplicate of cand_3eb93d212c60d3f2? But it's not exactly the same; it emphasizes the CO2 DCV recommendation. The first candidate was about reheat when min ventilation >25% or DCV used. This one is specifically about including heat with CO2 DCV. The evidence text is similar but not identical. Let's compare: first evidence: "If the minimum ventilation rate is more than 25% or so of the cooling maximum, or DCV is used, a reheat box is recommended to avoid overcooling. DCV logic is not provided for cooling-only boxes, because doing so results in periods of overcooling, as the CO2 levels due to occupants rises much faster than the cooling load due to occupants because of thermal mass." Second evidence: "CO 2 DCV for cooling-only zones can lead to overcooling due to the faster rise in CO 2 levels from people in the room versus the increase in cooling loads from people. Including heat in all zones with CO 2 DCV is therefore recommended." They are from the same section and convey similar information. Could be considered a duplicate. However, the titles and summaries are slightly different. One is about reheat when ventilation is high, the other about including heat with CO2 DCV. But the underlying evidence is overlapping. I need to check if it's a duplicate extraction. The guideline likely has one place where it says this. The extractor might have created two candidates from the same section. The source pages are the same? First candidate source pages: [73]. Second candidate: [73]. Both from page 73. So likely from the same paragraph. I should check if they are essentially the same knowledge. First candidate: "Recommendation for reheat when minimum ventilation is high" summary says if min ventilation >25% or DCV, reheat recommended. Second: "Recommendation to include heating with CO2 DCV" specifically says including heat in all zones with CO2 DCV. They might be two distinct pieces: one is a conditional recommendation based on min ventilation percentage, the other is a blanket recommendation for all CO2 DCV zones. But from the evidence, the first says: "If the minimum ventilation rate is more than 25%... or DCV is used, a reheat box is recommended..." The second says: "CO2 DCV for cooling-only zones can lead to overcooling... Including heat in all zones with CO2 DCV is therefore recommended." They are conveying the same basic point: with DCV, include reheat/heat. The second is more specific to CO2 DCV. But they are from the same page and likely the same source text. I suspect it's a duplicate extraction. The instruction says: "Reject ... duplicated/noisy extraction". So I should flag the second as duplicate. But to be safe, I'll check if they have the same evidence. They don't have exactly the same quote. The second quote is different but also from page 73. It's plausible the guideline has both statements, but they might be part of the same paragraph. I'll treat them as distinct if they are different recommendations. However, they both say essentially the same thing: use reheat/heat with DCV. It could be that the first candidate has a broader condition (also >25% min vent), while the second only mentions CO2 DCV. So they are not identical. The first includes the >25% condition. The second omits that. But they are both from page 73. I need to decide if it's a duplicate. Duplicate means same content extracted twice. The content is similar but not identical. The second quote starts with "CO 2 DCV for cooling-only zones can lead to overcooling..." That might be a different sentence. Let's see: The first quote ends with "...thermal mass." The second quote seems to be a paraphrase of the same idea. Possibly it's from the same paragraph but worded differently. I think it's a duplicate extraction because they are both from the same very short section and give the same recommendation. The source pages are exactly [73], so likely the same snippet. If there were two distinct recommendations, the extractor might have split them. But they both talk about including heat/reheat due to DCV. So I'd reject the second as duplicate. However, to be thorough, I'll check if the second candidate's evidence is exactly a different sentence. It might be that the original text says: "If the minimum ventilation rate is more than 25% or so of the cooling maximum, or DCV is used, a reheat box is recommended to avoid overcooling." Then later: "CO2 DCV for cooling-only zones can lead to overcooling due to the faster rise in CO2 levels... Including heat in all zones with CO2 DCV is therefore recommended." That could be two sentences. But that's still the same point. The extractor split it into two pieces. They are both valid knowledge but they are redundant. The instruction says to reject duplicated extraction. So I'll mark the second as duplicate. I'll assign category_if_not: "duplicate" for cand_640de17e4ddea1b5. The first one is already accepted.

Now, are there any other duplicates? All other candidates seem distinct.

Check for generic definitions without operational use: none appear so.

Check for unsupported summaries: The evidence quotes support the summaries. All good.

Thus, verdicts: all true except the last one false for duplicate.

I'll output JSON.
